personality_voice.book_baseline: Filch is defined by a cluster of tightly consistent traits across all seven books: sadistic glee at student suffering ('hard work and pain are the best teachers'), obsessive rule enforcement, wheezing physical presence, theatrical menace, and a bitter resentment rooted in being a Squib. His speech is characteristically sputtering and self-interrupting when flustered (the Kwikspell scene: 'If I thought you'd read my private - not that it's mine - for a friend - be that as it may'), and gleefully cruel when in control ('I'll be back at dawn, for what's left of them'). He is also genuinely pathetic in moments of vulnerability - his dry racking sobs over Mrs. Norris, his desperate excitement at Umbridge's Educational Decrees. His voice alternates between theatrical menace and sputtering impotence.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The films preserve the core personality well. Film Filch delivers the detention speech with authentic sadistic relish ('A pity they let the old punishments die. There was a time detention would find you hanging by your thumbs in the dungeons. God, I miss the screaming'). His wheezing, flat-footed pursuit, his devotion to Mrs. Norris, his gleeful cruelty toward students, and his sputtering outrage at Mrs. Norris's Petrification are all present. The Kwikspell embarrassment is retained in CoS. In OotP, his alliance with Umbridge and excitement at the prospect of whipping students is shown. The DH scene ('Students out of bed! Students in the corridors!' followed by 'Oh. Sorry, mum.') captures his oblivious buffoonery.
personality_voice.difference: The films lose the sustained texture of Filch's voice across many scenes. His sputtering, self-interrupting speech pattern when flustered is present in CoS but largely absent elsewhere. Several scenes that show his theatrical cruelty in extended form (the walk to the Forbidden Forest in PS, the Dungbomb confrontation in OotP, his prowling with a horsewhip after Fred and George leave) are compressed or absent. The film Filch is slightly more comic and slightly less genuinely menacing than the book version, where his sadism has more weight because it is sustained across hundreds of pages.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The main traits - sadism, rule obsession, Squib bitterness, devotion to Mrs. Norris, theatrical cruelty, wheezing physicality - are all present and recognizable. The loss is one of sustained texture and the full range of his voice (particularly the flustered, self-interrupting register). This warrants a minor deduction but not a substantial one, as the dominant pattern is preserved.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Filch serves multiple narrative functions across the books: (1) primary obstacle to student rule-breaking, creating tension in pursuit scenes across PS, CoS, PoA, GoF, OotP, HBP; (2) institutional enforcer whose presence defines Hogwarts as a place with real consequences; (3) comic foil to Peeves; (4) instrument of Umbridge's regime in OotP; (5) unwitting revealer of his own Squib status, which adds pathos and explains his bitterness; (6) minor plot function in GoF (the egg scene with Snape and Moody). He actively pursues, threatens, reports, and patrols - he is a recurring agent of danger.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The films preserve his role as obstacle and institutional enforcer. His pursuit scenes in PS and CoS are present. His alliance with Umbridge in OotP is shown. His role in the Battle of Hogwarts (leading Slytherins to the dungeons, then being terrified by the giant) is present. The GoF egg scene is absent from the film corpus provided, though Filch does appear at the Yule Ball. His function as Peeves's antagonist is largely lost because Peeves was cut from the films entirely.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The most significant agency loss is the complete removal of Peeves from the films, which eliminates an entire dimension of Filch's narrative function - his ongoing war with Peeves, which generates multiple plot-relevant scenes (the Kwikspell distraction in PS, the trophy room chase, the staircase scene). Filch's role as the primary pursuit threat is also somewhat reduced across the later films as the Marauder's Map and Invisibility Cloak scenes are compressed. His GoF egg-scene agency (the confrontation with Snape and Moody on the staircase, his yearningly wanting to catch Peeves) is absent from the film corpus. His OotP role as Umbridge's instrument is preserved but his active prowling with a horsewhip and his role in the swamp punishment are reduced.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The Peeves removal is a structural loss that eliminates a recurring narrative function. However, Filch's core role as obstacle and enforcer is maintained across the films. The penalty is moderate - his function is recognizable but meaningfully reduced in scope.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Filch's motivations are unusually coherent for a minor character: his cruelty toward students stems directly from his Squib status - he cannot do what they can, he resents them for it, and he compensates by wielding the only power available to him (institutional authority, physical surveillance, punishment). His excitement at Umbridge's decrees is explicitly motivated by the prospect of finally being allowed to punish students physically. His devotion to Mrs. Norris is his only genuine emotional attachment. The Kwikspell letter reveals an internal conflict: he wants to be a wizard, he is ashamed of not being one, and he is humiliated when this is exposed.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The Kwikspell motivation is preserved in CoS - the film retains the letter, his embarrassment, and Ron's explanation that his hatred of students stems from bitterness at being a Squib. His excitement at Umbridge's decrees in OotP is shown. His devotion to Mrs. Norris is present throughout. The core motivational structure is intact.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The films do not develop the Squib motivation beyond the CoS scene. In the books, this motivation is reinforced repeatedly - his reaction to Neville's comment about Squibs being targeted, Malfoy's use of 'filthy Squib' as an insult (which Filch cannot respond to magically), and the ongoing texture of his powerlessness. The film Filch's motivations are present but underexplored after CoS.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: The motivations are established and not contradicted. The simplification is one of depth rather than accuracy - the films get the 'why' right but don't sustain it. This is a minor penalty.

character_arc.book_baseline: Filch has a deliberately flat arc - he is the same bitter, cruel, rule-obsessed caretaker in book 1 as in book 7. The only meaningful development is his temporary empowerment under Umbridge (OotP), which is then reversed when she leaves, leaving him 'really, really miserable' and saying 'Umbridge was the best thing that ever happened to Hogwarts.' His final appearance in DH shows him in his habitual role, briefly given a task (leading Slytherins to the dungeons) before being terrified by a giant - a fitting comic-pathetic ending consistent with his entire characterization. The arc is intentionally flat.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The films preserve the flat arc. Filch in DH is recognizably the same character as in PS. The Umbridge empowerment and subsequent deflation is present in OotP. His DH appearance (the 'Students out of bed' scene followed by being corrected by McGonagall) is consistent with his established characterization.
character_arc.difference: The post-Umbridge deflation ('Filch is really, really miserable... he keeps saying Umbridge was the best thing that ever happened to Hogwarts') is absent from the film corpus, though this is a minor scene. The flat arc is otherwise preserved.
character_arc.penalty_logic: A flat arc preserved as flat warrants no significant penalty. The Umbridge aftermath is a small loss but does not alter the arc's trajectory. Score reflects the arc being compressed but faithful.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Filch's two defining relationships are: (1) Mrs. Norris - his only genuine emotional bond, treated as a beloved companion and extension of himself; her Petrification devastates him in a way that generates genuine pathos even from characters who despise him. (2) Peeves - his eternal antagonist, a relationship that generates recurring comic and plot-relevant scenes throughout the series. Secondary relationships include his alliance with Umbridge (OotP) and his antagonism toward students generally, particularly Harry.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The Mrs. Norris relationship is well-preserved - his horror at her Petrification, his sobs, his devotion are all present in CoS. His alliance with Umbridge is shown in OotP. His general antagonism toward Harry is consistent. However, the Peeves relationship is entirely absent because Peeves was cut from the films.
key_relationships.difference: The Peeves relationship is a total loss. In the books, Filch's war with Peeves is one of his most consistent characterizing relationships - it generates the Kwikspell distraction, the trophy room chase, the staircase scene in PS, the Christmas armor scene in GoF, and multiple other moments. Peeves is also the one entity that consistently defeats and humiliates Filch, which is essential to understanding Filch's powerlessness. This is a significant relational loss.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The Mrs. Norris relationship is preserved well. The Peeves relationship is entirely absent. This is a meaningful loss that changes the emotional texture of Filch's characterization - his ongoing humiliation by Peeves is part of what makes him pathetic rather than merely cruel. The score reflects this as a notable but not catastrophic loss, since Mrs. Norris (his primary relationship) is intact.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Filch's complexity comes from several sources: (1) the Squib pathos - he is genuinely pitiable, a failed wizard in a world of magic, compensating through petty authority; (2) the Kwikspell letter - a moment of genuine vulnerability and shame that complicates his cruelty; (3) his relationship with Peeves, which shows him as perpetually defeated by the one entity he cannot punish; (4) his reaction to Mrs. Norris's Petrification, which generates real sympathy; (5) his excitement under Umbridge followed by his misery after her departure, showing that his cruelty is rooted in genuine deprivation; (6) small humanizing details - his moldy tailcoat at the Christmas feast, his rheumatism, his filing cabinet of punishments, his chains kept 'well oiled in case they're ever needed.'
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The films preserve the Kwikspell pathos (CoS), the Mrs. Norris devotion, the Umbridge alliance, and the physical characterization (wheezing, flat-footed, jowls aquiver). The DH scene adds a moment of comic pathos. However, the sustained texture of his complexity across seven books is necessarily compressed.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The Peeves relationship (a major source of his comic-pathetic complexity) is entirely absent. The post-Umbridge misery scene is absent. The extended GoF egg-staircase scene, which shows Filch's competing desires (catching Peeves vs. helping Snape) and his plaintive negotiating with authority figures, is absent from the film corpus. The filing cabinet, the chains, the detailed description of his office - the physical texture of his obsession - are largely absent. Several scenes showing his sustained prowling and petty cruelty (the Dungbomb confrontation in OotP, the horsewhip prowling after Fred and George) are reduced.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The films preserve the core of Filch's complexity - the Squib pathos, the Mrs. Norris devotion, the sadistic glee - but lose the sustained texture that makes him more than a one-note villain. The Peeves absence is the most significant loss. The character remains recognizable and substantially characterized, but the richness of the book version is noticeably reduced.

lost_or_transferred_material: Entire Peeves-Filch antagonist relationship (recurring across PS, CoS, PoA, GoF, OotP, HBP) - Peeves cut from films entirely
GoF staircase scene: Filch's competing motivations (catching Peeves vs. helping Snape), his plaintive negotiation, his reluctant surrender of the egg to Moody
Post-Umbridge misery: Filch saying 'Umbridge was the best thing that ever happened to Hogwarts' and being 'really, really miserable' after her departure
Extended OotP material: Filch prowling corridors with horsewhip, punting students across Fred and George's swamp, the full texture of his role as Umbridge's instrument
Physical texture of Filch's office: chains kept oiled, filing cabinet of punishments, Fred and George's dedicated drawer
Dungbomb confrontation in OotP Owlery: Filch's self-satisfied hiss, his being faced down by Cho, his retreating impotently
Filch's role in securing the castle after Sirius Black's break-in (PoA) - reduced to a single line in the film corpus
The sustained pattern of Filch being outwitted and humiliated across multiple books, which establishes his fundamental powerlessness
score_caps_applied: Cap 2 considered (above 80 if key relationships change substantially): Peeves relationship is entirely absent, Mrs. Norris relationship is preserved - partial application; score kept below 80 on this basis
Cap 1 considered (above 85 if major complexity removed): Peeves absence and sustained texture loss prevent score above 80
comparative.key_observations: Filch is one of the more faithfully adapted minor characters in the film series. His core identity - sadistic Squib caretaker, devoted to Mrs. Norris, bitter toward students, gleefully cruel - is preserved across all eight films with consistent voice and physicality. The primary losses are structural rather than characterizational: the complete removal of Peeves eliminates Filch's most important antagonist relationship and a recurring source of his comic-pathetic complexity, while the compression of later films removes the sustained texture of his petty cruelty and powerlessness. The Kwikspell pathos and Mrs. Norris devotion - the two elements that give him genuine depth - are both preserved. The result is a recognizable and substantially faithful portrayal that loses nuance through absence of material rather than through mischaracterization.
